front: Sculpture: equestrian statue completed by Alessandro Leopardi after the sculptor's death
back: Equestrian Statue of Bartolomeo Colleoni (Andrea del Verrocchio)

front: Sculpture: pedestal has three pillars on each long side, referencing rumor that the subject had three testicles
back: Equestrian Statue of Bartolomeo Colleoni (Andrea del Verrocchio)

front: Sculpture: sculptor destroyed the head in rage; when threatened with beheading, replied he could return a head to a statue but not to his own body
back: Equestrian Statue of Bartolomeo Colleoni (Andrea del Verrocchio)

front: Sculpture: Frick Collection holds a sculpture by this artist attributed to the wrong family due to a mistake over the testicle motif
back: Equestrian Statue of Bartolomeo Colleoni (Andrea del Verrocchio)

front: Painting: Vasari claimed this sculptor gave up painting after an apprentice drew an angel in this painting more beautifully than any prior work
back: Baptism of Christ (Andrea del Verrocchio and Leonardo da Vinci)

front: Painting: Leonardo painted the angel at the left of this painting executed by his teacher
back: Baptism of Christ (Andrea del Verrocchio and Leonardo da Vinci)

front: Sculpture: narrative bronze for a niche in Orsanmichele depicting one figure raising his right hand above another
back: Christ and Saint Thomas (Andrea del Verrocchio)

front: Sculpture: bronze figure wearing a leather tunic, standing on Goliath's head, housed in the Palazzo Vecchio
Sculpture: this depiction of David shows flowers covering his chest and pseudo-Arabic script on his tunic
Sculpture: a putto raises a leg in the air and holds a fork-tailed dolphin in a bronze sculpture in the Palazzo Vecchio
Artist: trained as a goldsmith before turning to sculpture and painting
Sculpture: first marble bust to include the subject's hands
Artist: made La Piagnona, the bell used by Savonarola that was put on trial and flogged
Artist: collaborated with Michelozzo on the Florence Baptistry's silver altar of San Giovanni
Artist: collaborated with wax-painting specialist Orsino Benintendi to create three life-size figures of the targets of the Pazzi Conspiracy
Artist: created statue of marble, porphyry, and bronze to commemorate Piero and Giovanni de Medici
Artist: introduced the presentation of Madonna, child, and saints framed by a loggia and landscape views in an altarpiece for Pistoia Cathedral
Artist: designed the golden ball and cross atop the lantern of the Florence Cathedral (designed by Brunelleschi)

back: Equestrian Statue of Bartolomeo Colleoni (Andrea del Verrocchio)
